choices.text: Local mode mocks bindings (KV in-memory, R2 local disk, D1 local SQLite, secrets from .dev.vars). Fast iteration. --remote uses actual Cloudflare bindings (real KV/R2/D1) — slower but real data. Use local for most dev; --remote when you need to test against production data or verify integration.

questions.explanation: Local mocks bindings; --remote uses real Cloudflare bindings.

questions.prompt: Worker has 15 routes plus middleware. Raw fetch or Hono?

choices.text: Hono. Raw fetch handler with 15 routes becomes a long if/else chain with manual JSON parsing, repeated error handling, ad-hoc middleware. Hono (~10KB) gives Express-like routing, built-in middleware (CORS, auth, logger, security, cache, compression), context helpers, type-safe bindings via `<{ Bindings: Env }>` generic, and Zod-based validation via @hono/zod-validator. Bundle and runtime overhead minimal; DX win large. Standard 2026 choice for Workers APIs.

questions.explanation: Hono for any multi-route Worker. ~10KB; huge DX win.

questions.prompt: Why verify JWTs locally instead of via external API per request?

choices.text: Local verification is microseconds via Web Crypto; external API call is 50-200ms per request plus cost/rate limits. JWTs are self-validating — signature proves the issuer; payload claims are trustworthy if signature is valid. For HMAC use the shared secret; for RSA/ECDSA fetch JWKS once and cache. Hono's jwt middleware handles both. Reserve external API calls for cases needing real-time info beyond JWT claims.

questions.explanation: Local crypto is microseconds vs 50-200ms external. JWTs designed for self-validation.

questions.prompt: User profile cached for fast reads. Should refresh in background. Pattern?

choices.text: Stale-while-revalidate: check cache; if hit, serve immediately AND fire `ctx.waitUntil(refresh(...))` to update cache in background; if miss, compute synchronously, store, return. User always sees fast cached response; next request gets fresh data. waitUntil keeps isolate alive after response sent. Standard pattern for 'data should be fresh-ish but response must be fast.'

`httpOnly: true` (prevents JS access; XSS protection), `secure: true` (HTTPS only), `sameSite: 'Lax'` (CSRF protection), `path: '/'`, reasonable maxAge. For cross-origin: sameSite='None' + secure=true. Pair with CSRF middleware (hono/csrf) for cookie-based forms. Skipping any of these creates real vulnerabilities — non-httpOnly is the most common 'stolen session' attack vector.

questions.explanation: httpOnly + secure + sameSite='Lax'. Each blocks specific attack categories.
